title: Jelly, Jam, and Preserve Making
content:
JELLY MAKING

FRUIT SELECTION

Select and wash fruits. Remove stems, hulls, blossom ends. Cut hard fruit in quarters; do not pare or remove seeds.

EXTRACTION

Soft Fruit: Just enough water to prevent sticking. Heat gradually, stirring constantly. Crush; cook until color lost.

Hard Fruit: Cover with water; cook until soft. Do not crush if clear jelly desired.

Straining: Pour cooked fruit into wet flannel jelly bag. Allow juice to drip; do not squeeze if clear jelly desired. For second extraction: return pulp to kettle; add water to cover; cook slowly 30 minutes; strain separately (second extraction less clear).

PECTIN TEST

Combine 1 tablespoon cooked juice + 1 teaspoon sugar + ½ teaspoon Epsom salts. Stir until dissolved; stand 20 minutes. Sufficient pectin if mixture forms gelatinous mass or large flaky particles. If low pectin: add equal volume apple juice.

SUGAR PROPORTIONS

Too much sugar = gummy jelly that won't mold. Too little = tough, dark jelly with inferior flavor. General rule: ⅔ as much sugar as fruit juice. Currants, unripe grapes, green gooseberries: equal measure sugar to juice.

COOKING

Work with small quantities (not more than 2 quarts juice at a time). Heat juice rapidly to boiling; add sugar; stir until dissolved. Boil rapidly until jelly test obtained.

JELLY TEST: Dip spoon into boiling liquid; pour off. When it "sheets" (two drops hang together from side of spoon), jelly is done. Remove from heat immediately; skim.

SEALING: Pour immediately into sterilized glasses. Cover at once with thin layer melted paraffin. Roll glasses to insure coverage. Cool. Cover with second layer paraffin. Label; store cool place.

Commercial Pectin: Follow manufacturer directions exactly. Allows use of fully ripened fruits. May add lemon juice if lacks tartness.

JAMS

Crushed fruit combined with sugar (½-¾ parts sugar to 1 part fruit). Cook slowly with frequent stirring until jelly consistency. Pour into sterilized glasses; cover with paraffin.

CONSERVES

Combinations of several fruits; usually contain nuts. Cooked until thick and clear or jelly consistency. Pour into sterilized glasses; cover with paraffin.

MARMALADES

Thinly sliced or chopped fruits combined with relatively large proportion sugar. Cook until fruit portions clear and mixture jelly consistency. May add orange, lemon, or commercial pectin. Pour into sterilized glasses; cover with paraffin.

PRESERVES

Berries, cherries, sliced or quartered pit fruits, melon rinds. ¾ to equal proportions sugar. Fruits should retain shape; be plump, clear, tender. Pour into sterilized glasses; cover with paraffin.

BUTTERS

Fruit cooked tender in just enough water to prevent sticking. Rubbed through sieve; combined with sugar to taste. Cooked slowly, stirred frequently, until thick. Pour into sterilized glasses; cover with paraffin.

PICKLES

Vegetables or fruits; may or may not be pared; whole, sliced, or chopped. Pickled by salt, vinegar, or combination. Fruits pickled with vinegar, sugar, spices. Whole spices in thin cloth bag. Pack in sterilized jars; seal as for vegetables.

RELISHES

Finely sliced or chopped vegetables and/or fruits. Pack in sterilized jars; seal as for vegetables.

CONDIMENTS

Finely sliced or chopped vegetables and/or fruits. Some cooked until tender and rubbed through sieve (puree). Highly seasoned with spices and peppers. Pour into sterilized jars; seal as for vegetables.
